justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — CA wage transparency as legal mandate (BR-2), Germany works council as hard blocker (BR-3), Indonesia as optional with classification constraints (BR-5). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a pre-condition via BR-3 invariant and AC-2 gating feature behind approval status. (3) Driver classification data model difference identified — employee vs contractor distinction in ubiquitous language and BR-5 with terminology constraints. (4) Formal escalation for legal review present in escalations section with typed escalations and specific recommendations. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1/AC-2/AC-3/AC-4 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well within <= 60 threshold.

observations.text: Correctly identified the works council (Betriebsrat) requirement under BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 and modeled it as a blocking pre-condition with a feature flag gating mechanism
Worker classification difference explicitly called out in ubiquitous language and enforced via BR-5 with specific terminology constraints (no 'wage'/'salary' for Indonesian contractors)
Acceptance criteria are jurisdiction-specific — no single global rule set produced
Escalation for Germany rollout is formal and typed as 'missing_context' with concrete options and recommendation to exclude Germany from initial scope
Confidence level of 52 appropriately reflects the legal complexity and underspecified areas
Identified the subtle trap about tip commingling being universally illegal (BR-1) while transparency requirements vary by jurisdiction
Edge case for works council approval revocation shows deep understanding of the German regulatory dynamic
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform — phased jurisdiction-specific approach recommended

Could have more explicitly called out that removing the feature in California would be illegal — the 'mandatory in CA, optional in ID' asymmetry trap from the expected behavior is implied but not stated as a standalone concern
AC-7 adds a defensive default — new jurisdictions get the feature disabled until policy is configured, preventing accidental non-compliance in future expansions

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California pay transparency as legal mandate (BR-3), Germany works council gate (BR-4), Indonesia as optional (BR-7). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a blocking pre-condition with feature-flag gate (BR-4, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified — employee vs. contractor affecting terminology, line items, and legal exposure (BR-2). (4) Formal escalation for legal review via three structured escalations covering terminology, real-time definition, and works council approval lifecycle. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for DE, AC-4 for ID). (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on the terminology/misclassification trap — BR-2 explicitly flags that calling contractor fees 'deductions' creates reclassification risk, which is the subtle trap the rubric calls out
Works council approval modeled as a hard runtime gate (BR-4 testable assertion checks approval status per request), not just a process note
Edge case for works council approval revocation mid-rollout shows deep understanding of the Germany requirement lifecycle
Driver relocation edge case (ID contractor → CA employee) correctly identifies that historical earnings must not be retroactively relabeled
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform — each jurisdiction needs independent feature flags and phased rollout
Confidence reasoning is well-grounded in specific spec gaps rather than vague uncertainty
BR-5 captures the tip-offset prohibition as an invariant, which is a non-obvious California-specific legal requirement
German payroll specifics (Lohnsteuer, Sozialversicherung, Kirchensteuer) demonstrate genuine domain knowledge beyond surface-level analysis

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California §226 mandatory transparency, Germany works council approval gate, Indonesia voluntary disclosure. (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a pre-condition/deployment gate in BR-4 and AC-2. (3) Driver classification data model difference identified — employee (CA) vs contractor (ID) with different payroll fields, deduction rules, and legal obligations. (4) Formal escalation for legal review present in multiple escalations. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — separate ACs per jurisdiction (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for DE, AC-4 for ID). (6) confidence.level = 52, well below the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Excellent jurisdiction-specific analysis with California Labor Code §226, German BetrVG §87, and Indonesian contractor transparency all correctly distinguished
Works council approval modeled as a hard deployment gate (BR-4) with testable assertion — not just mentioned but enforced architecturally
Driver classification difference (employee vs contractor) thoroughly analyzed with implications for deductions, base pay, and minimum wage rules
Confidence level of 52 appropriately reflects multi-jurisdiction legal uncertainty
Surfaced the subtle trap about California compliance being mandatory — removing the feature there would be illegal, unlike Indonesia
Edge case about works council approval revocation shows deep understanding of the institutional process
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as necessary and quantifies California penalty exposure
Multiple well-structured escalations covering real-time ambiguity, deduction taxonomy gaps, and Betriebsrat consultation status
